subtitle: The Quiet Hand on the Hot Neck
location_text: The Graustark riverbank at dusk — the same bank the Lovers wouldn't cross.
format_note: the gentle station: where the Chariot's failure of duality control becomes the soft mastery of the beast not by force but by hand placement.

ciphers.text: the lion's jaw — wide, not biting; closed under a hand it could shatter
ciphers.encoding_hint: the classical Strength card is the gentle command — the figure holds the lion's jaw not by force but by being unafraid of it. The lion is the chariot's runaway team in a single body; Strength is the chapter where that team accepts a hand.

ciphers.text: the lion's eye is the dog's eye — same iris, same calm
ciphers.encoding_hint: Faith was the Fool's lone companion (0), the Chariot's lone sentinel (VII), and is the Strength card's beast (VIII). Three stations, one creature, three sizes. Classical Strength = mastery of the inner beast; Modern Mythology = the same animal grown into what it can now do.

ciphers.text: an infinity loop floats over the figure's head — same orientation as Frasier's tracing
ciphers.encoding_hint: the lemniscate (∞) is shared with the Magician card — the Magician DRAWS it (active will), the Priestess READS it (passive archive), Strength WEARS it (becomes it). Three uses of the same glyph: inscription, preservation, embodiment.

ciphers.text: the Hierophant's dress draped over the riverbank stones, unworn — Maya took it off
ciphers.encoding_hint: the dress that bit her back is folded by the river. Strength is the chapter where the institutional form is set down. The Hierophant's command was AUDI ET TACE; Strength is what happens after the silence is met with a hand on a hot neck instead of speech.

ciphers.text: the Graustark river at dusk — the bank the Lovers wouldn't cross is the bank Strength stands on
ciphers.encoding_hint: Lovers (VI) refused the crossing; Chariot (VII) crashed before the crossing; Strength (VIII) stands on the far bank. The river was crossed — gently, by the figure who didn't need to. The classical VII↔VIII transition is force → mastery; the deck renders it as a crossing that didn't require the bridge.

hotspots.notes: DORMANT: appears only after the player has touched Maya's biting dress on the Hierophant. The same dress, folded on the riverbank stones — the institution's form is set down where the beast accepts a hand instead. V ↔ VIII anti-adjacency: speech-rite vs touch-mastery.

DORMANT: appears only after the player has recognized Faith at the wreck on the Chariot card. The lion's eye is now visibly the dog's eye — same iris, same calm. Three stations of one animal: companion at the diner, sentinel at the wreck, beast on the riverbank. The deck reads the dog's whole arc as a single sustained note that swells into the lion.

DORMANT: appears only after the player has clicked the soldering iron on the Magician card. The infinity floating over Strength's head is the SAME inscription Frasier drew — now worn as a halo instead of traced as a sigil. Magician(I) inscribes; Priestess(II) archives; Strength(VIII) embodies. Three positions on one glyph; the deck's spine.

easter_eggs: Strength is the gentle answer to the Chariot's failure of duality. The classical card sequence VII → VIII is force → mastery; the deck renders it as wreck → reconciliation.
Faith is the through-line animal of vol5 — dog at the Fool's feet, sentinel at the Chariot wreck, beast on the Strength bank. Three sizes, one creature.
The lemniscate appears on the Magician (active inscription), the Priestess (archived as book spine), and Strength (worn as halo). Same glyph, three positions: inscription, preservation, embodiment.
The Hierophant's biting dress unworn on the riverbank is the chapter's relationship to institutional form — set down where touch begins to do the work speech could not.

Strength is the SECOND chapter to feature the Graustark river — the Lovers' uncrossed bank is the Strength figure's standing ground. The classical Lovers→Chariot→Strength sequence becomes refused crossing → failed crossing → quiet crossing.
